figures.gloss: The supreme sovereign of the Carthaginian pantheon, an aging, bearded agricultural and solar deity who presided over fertility and the ultimate covenants of the state. He is the divine recipient of the city's most solemn vows, appeased through the fiery rites of the tophet.
figures.facet: Unlike the dynamic, storm-wielding Levantine Baal, Hammon is a static, brooding patriarch. His power lies not in martial combat, but in the terrifying, absolute enforcement of elite vows and agricultural cycles.
figures.provenance: Stele dedications at the Carthage Tophet (precinct of Salammbo); treaty of Hannibal with Philip V of Macedon.

figures.gloss: The paramount goddess of Carthage, eclipsing her consort Baal Hammon in late Punic history. Titled 'Pene Baal' (Face of Baal), she is a celestial, lunar, and apotropaic mother deity who guided maritime navigation and fiercely protected the city's domestic sanctuaries.
figures.facet: She aggressively absorbs the roles of earlier Levantine goddesses, stripping away their mythic narratives to become a purely abstract, geometric emblem of Carthaginian imperial and maternal protection.
figures.provenance: Thousands of votive stelae at the Carthage Tophet; sanctuary at Sarepta; Thinissut terracotta figures.

figures.gloss: The tutelary god of Tyre transplanted to the Carthaginian empire. He is a dying-and-rising deity whose annual awakening ('egersis') renewed the cosmos. As a relentless colonial patron, he safeguarded maritime trade networks and Punic expansion across the Mediterranean.
figures.facet: He is a deity of sheer mercantile imperialism. His awakening did not merely secure agricultural fertility; it synchronized with the sailing season, binding divine resurrection directly to Carthaginian naval hegemony.
figures.provenance: Bilingual cippi of Malta; treaty of Hannibal; accounts by Polybius and Diodorus Siculus.

figures.gloss: The chief god of healing and renewal, headquartered at the Byrsa hill in Carthage. Originally a beautiful youth who castrated himself to escape the goddess Astarte's advances, he was resurrected by her restorative heat and elevated to divine physician.
figures.facet: He intertwines physical healing with radical bodily mutilation. The restoration of health requires a primordial trauma; his cult emphasizes the fragile, easily broken boundary between mortal suffering and divine immortality.
figures.provenance: Temple of Eshmun at Byrsa (Carthage); the Eshmunazar II sarcophagus inscription; Damascius's 'Life of Isidore'.

figures.gloss: The open-air sanctuary where Carthaginians cremated infants and young animals and interred the calcined remains in urns beneath inscribed 'mlk' (votive) stelae to Baal Hammon and Tanit. Ancient Greek and Roman writers (Diodorus, Cleitarchus, later Tertullian) describe the practice as child sacrifice; a live modern scholarly debate (Xella and others vs. Schwartz, Smith, Stager, among others) contests whether some or all of the interments instead reflect an infant cemetery for the stillborn and naturally dead. The evidence does not currently settle the question.
figures.facet: It systematizes what ancient sources call the ultimate human cost of divine favor into a bureaucratic civic duty — though whether the underlying practice was sacrifice or infant burial custom remains a live, unresolved scholarly dispute.
figures.provenance: Archaeological excavations at Salammbo (Carthage), Motya, and Tharros; Diodorus Siculus (Bibliotheca historica 20.14); Cleitarchus.

figures.gloss: A ubiquitous, abstract geometric symbol consisting of a triangle, a horizontal crossbar, and a circle at the apex. It functioned as an apotropaic seal marking thresholds, graves, and votive stelae, physically rendering the goddess Tanit's presence without idolatry.
figures.facet: It rejects the anthropomorphism of classical antiquity. In a Mediterranean awash in sculpted human gods, Carthaginian devotion crystallizes into an irreducible, aniconic schematic of divine geometry.
figures.provenance: Stelae at the Carthage Tophet; Punic domestic mosaics (Kerkouane); Carthaginian coinage.

figures.gloss: A Levantine (Ugaritic/Phoenician) master-craftsman god — Kothar-wa-Hasis, 'skilled and wise' — credited in Levantine sources with inventing ironworking, magical weapons, and seafaring technology. His specifically Punic (western, Carthaginian-era) attestation is thin: the record here rests on inherited Levantine literary transmission rather than western Punic inscriptions.
figures.facet: He represents the technological pragmatism of the Phoenician-Punic diaspora. His divine magic is not mystical, but explicitly industrial and nautical—engineering the physical tools that made Mediterranean trade networks possible.
figures.provenance: Ugaritic Baal Cycle (KTU 1.1–1.6); Sanchuniathon via Philo of Byblos (Levantine transmission — Punic-specific epigraphic attestation not established).

figures.gloss: The storm-god of Mount Zaphon (Jebel al-Aqra), patron of seafarers and safe harbor — distinct from the sky-god Baal Shamem ('Lord of the Heavens'), with whom loose summaries sometimes conflate him. He appears as a solemn witness-deity in the Hannibal–Philip V treaty, invoked with the sun, moon, and other divine powers to guarantee the oath.
figures.facet: He is the theological embodiment of the horizon. As Carthage pushed past the Pillars of Hercules, this sky-god became an active maritime navigator, turning unpredictable weather into a structured domain.
figures.provenance: Treaty between Hannibal and Philip V of Macedon (recorded by Polybius 7.9).

figures.gloss: The great Phoenician goddess of love, war, and the sea, carried west by the colonists; prominent in Punic devotion especially at Eryx in Sicily ('Astarte Erycina') and attested in the bilingual Pyrgi Tablets from the Punic-Etruscan port sanctuary. At Carthage itself she was progressively eclipsed by, and absorbed into, Tanit ('Pene Baal') by the mid-first millennium BCE.
figures.facet: She shows how a pantheon actually consolidates: rather than vanishing, her cult sites and functions migrate into Tanit's expanding portfolio, visible archaeologically as a slow overlay rather than a single replacement event.
figures.provenance: Pyrgi Tablets (bilingual Phoenician-Etruscan dedication, Caere, 5th c. BCE); Punic dedications at Eryx recorded by Diodorus Siculus; KAI (Kanaanäische und Aramäische Inschriften) corpus.

figures.gloss: The Tyrian princess who, per the legend, fled Tyre after her brother Pygmalion murdered her husband, sailed west with a company of nobles, and founded Carthage on land bought through the 'oxhide' ruse of cutting a bull's hide into strips to encircle the Byrsa hill. Ancient sources hold she died on a pyre by her own hand rather than remarry the Libyan king Iarbas. Evidence for an actual Carthaginian civic cult of Elissa is thin; this entry claims only the founding legend.
figures.facet: Carthage's own foundation charter runs through a queen rather than a god — a civic legend of exile and self-sovereignty, not a theogony; only later Roman poetry (Virgil) recasts her death as heartbreak over Aeneas rather than resistance to remarriage.
figures.provenance: Timaeus of Tauromenium (fragments, via later citation); Justin, Epitome of Pompeius Trogus, Historiae Philippicae Book 18; Virgil, Aeneid (poetic elaboration, not a Punic source).

figures.gloss: A Punic god venerated chiefly at the temple of Antas in southwestern Sardinia, attested in Punic and neo-Punic inscriptions naming him 'Sid Addir Babi.' When Rome rebuilt the same sanctuary, it identified him with Sardus Pater, the legendary ancestor-founder of the Sardinians — a Roman equation, not necessarily Sid's own Punic self-understanding.
figures.facet: He is evidence that Punic religion extended beyond Carthage proper into a genuine provincial network of local cults, each rooted to its own sanctuary, which Rome later reinterpreted rather than erased.
figures.provenance: Punic and neo-Punic inscriptions from the Antas temple (Corpus Inscriptionum Semiticarum I); Sardinian archaeological reports on the Antas sanctuary.

figures.gloss: 'Shed the healer' — a Phoenician-Punic protective and healing god, attested in theophoric personal names and dedications across the Punic west. At Leptis Magna his cult was assimilated under Roman rule to Liber Pater (Dionysus), giving the city its own version of the wine-and-license god alongside Rome's pantheon.
figures.facet: He duplicates and regionalizes Eshmun's healing function — Punic religion did not have one empire-wide healer-god but several, each anchored to a particular city's sanctuary.
figures.provenance: Theophoric Punic personal names in Punic and neo-Punic epigraphy; Leptis Magna dedications equating him with Liber Pater, recorded in the Inscriptions of Roman Tripolitania (IRT).

figures.gloss: In 396 BCE, after Himilco's army sacked the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore near Syracuse, plague broke out in the Carthaginian camp; reading this as divine anger, the Carthaginian state formally adopted the Greek cult, built temples, and installed priests of Greek descent to conduct the rites in Greek fashion alongside the existing pantheon.
figures.facet: It is rare hard evidence for pantheon change as a documented political act rather than myth — Carthage did not merely absorb a foreign goddess informally but legislated her cult into the state religion as reparative diplomacy with the gods.
figures.provenance: Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca historica 14.63 and 14.77 (recording events of 396 BCE).

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The supreme dyad of the late Carthaginian empire, jointly receiving the vows of the state.
Intra-tradition: The sacrificial rite and the patriarchal sovereign who demanded and received its terrifying calculus.
Intra-tradition: The goddess and her irreducible, aniconic geometric signature.
GOLD SEAM: Implacable, terrifying sovereign deities linked to wheels, fire, and the reception of ultimate human sacrifices in classical historiography.
GOLD SEAM: Ancient, bearded Levantine patriarchs who rule through covenants, demanding absolute obedience and fire-offerings from their chosen people.
GOLD SEAM: Egyptian and Punic syncretism; Tanit heavily absorbed Isis's iconography, including the solar disc and the knot, to signify universal maternal power.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme, astral Queens of Heaven who eclipsed their male counterparts to become the fiercely protective patrons of vast urban empires.
GOLD SEAM: Pre-eminent celestial goddesses of Semitic trading networks, often venerated through aniconic stones or abstract symbols rather than human statuary.
GOLD SEAM: Dying-and-rising gods whose annual awakening ('egersis') ensures the renewal of the earthly and agricultural order.
GOLD SEAM: Restless, pioneering deities of masculine expansion, who clear the path for civilization, industry, and the violent expansion of empires.
GOLD SEAM: Youthful, earth-bound deities associated with the sudden, miraculous revelation of hidden sacred knowledge and physical healing.
GOLD SEAM: Divine recipients of institutionalized child sacrifice, where the state enforces a brutal, mathematical exchange of elite blood for rain, fertility, and cosmic stability.
GOLD SEAM: Directly entangled in biblical history; the Punic 'mlk' (votive offering) was demonized by Israelite prophets into the monstrous idol 'Moloch', marking a violent theological schism.
GOLD SEAM: Historically syncretized as Zeus Kasios, blending the Levantine lord of the coastal storms with the Hellenic sky-father to protect Mediterranean sailors.
GOLD SEAM: Sovereign lords of the deep and the heavens who act as the ultimate, inescapable witnesses to treaties and oaths.
GOLD SEAM: Brilliant, limping divine artisans who forge magical weapons and autonomous technological marvels for the ruling gods.
GOLD SEAM: The Carthaginian amulets of the dwarf-craftsman Pataikos explicitly syncretized Levantine smith-god traditions with the Memphite creator Ptah.
GOLD SEAM: Both reduce the vastness of the cosmos and the divine presence into a single, highly formalized geometric schema that mirrors a stylized human form.
GOLD SEAM: Gods whose bodily mutilation and subsequent resurrection by a grieving goddess form the theological basis for human salvation and healing.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both are the paramount goddess-consort of a West Semitic storm-god's pantheon, sea-associated and maternal; the historical descent from Athirat to Tanit remains debated among scholars, so the link is kept structural rather than genealogical.

the Punic myth of Eshmun's death and restoration turns on Astarte's revivifying heat — the same goddess worshipped independently at Ugarit continuing into the Phoenician-Punic pantheon under her own name.
Phoenician and Punic Melqart was explicitly identified with Heracles across the Mediterranean — the 'Herakles' worshipped at Tyre, Gades and Carthage.
Intra-tradition: the Eshmun myth itself turns on Astarte — the youth's self-mutilation flees her advances, and her restorative heat resurrects him as the divine physician.
astral goddesses of love and war whose cult titles and iconography (the Queen of Heaven complex) traveled across the whole Semitic Near East.
Intra-tradition: parallel Punic healer-gods rooted to different cities (Leptis Magna vs. Carthage/Byrsa) rather than a single shared cult.
at Leptis Magna, Shadrapa was formally equated under Rome with Liber Pater/Dionysus, epigraphically attested in the Inscriptions of Roman Tripolitania.
Diodorus records Carthage's literal, state-sanctioned adoption of the Sicilian Greek cult of Demeter after 396 BCE — a documented historical transplant, not a myth-parallel.
the same 396 BCE adoption installed Kore/Persephone's cult alongside her mother's in the Carthaginian state religion.
after Carthage's fall Roman Africa continued Tanit's cult under the name Juno Caelestis, a documented provincial assimilation of the Punic 'Face of Baal' into the Roman state goddess.

The same storm-god of Mount Ṣapān carried west by Phoenician colonization: Ugarit's dynastic king-of-the-gods who defeats Sea and Death continues at Carthage as a treaty-witness and guarantor of navigation, invoked by name in Punic treaty texts — the myth shed, the jurisdiction kept.

dossier.essence: The religion of Carthage and the wider Punic (western Phoenician) world of the western Mediterranean — North Africa, Sardinia, western Sicily, Ibiza, and southern Spain — from Carthage's traditional founding (814 BCE) to its destruction by Rome in 146 BCE. A Levantine-derived polytheism, it centered on the divine couple Baal Hammon and the goddess Tanit and is notorious, in ancient and modern accounts alike, for the tophet child-sacrifice rite. It is a dead tradition, known chiefly from inscriptions and archaeology.
dossier.cosmos: The pantheon adapts a Phoenician inheritance to the west: Baal Hammon, the aging solar-and-agricultural sovereign, and Tanit ('Pene Baal,' Face of Baal), the celestial mother-goddess who eclipsed him in later Carthage; alongside Melqart, the patron of Tyre whose annual 'awakening' (egersis) renewed the world and shadowed Punic colonization, and the healer Eshmun, with Astarte and a storm-and-heaven Baal. Punic religion is recovered far more through cult than through myth, its narratives largely lost.
dossier.practice: It was a religion of vow and offering — votive stelae record vows paid to the gods — culminating in the tophet, an open-air precinct where the cremated remains of infants (and young-animal substitutes) were buried in urns beneath dedicatory stelae to Baal Hammon and Tanit, understood by ancient authors and much modern scholarship as child sacrifice. Temple priesthoods regulated offerings (as in the surviving sacrificial 'tariffs'), devotion crystallized in the aniconic 'Sign of Tanit,' and Melqart's egersis marked the ritual year.
dossier.canon: No scripture or literature survives — Carthage's books were dispersed at its fall, only Mago's agricultural treatise passing on in translation — so the sources are epigraphic (votive inscriptions and sacrificial tariffs) and archaeological, supplemented by hostile Greco-Roman writers and the Hebrew Bible's 'Moloch' polemic.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds covenant sealed by sacrifice — a religion of vow and payment in which the community's ultimate offering was formalized at the tophet into a civic institution — and an aniconic devotion striking amid a Mediterranean of sculpted gods.
sources: Shelby Brown, 'Late Carthaginian Child Sacrifice and Sacrificial Monuments in their Mediterranean Context' (1991)
Josephine Crawley Quinn, 'In Search of the Phoenicians' (2018)
